title: restart_game drops the `ai` flag on rematch — zero test coverage
evidence: Line 128–134: `newPlayers` is built by mapping `players.map(p => ({ id: p.id, name: p.name, color: p.color, ready: true, lastSeen: nowMs }))` — the `ai` field present on `StoredPlayer` is never spread or forwarded. No test file exists for restart_game (no .test.ts in supabase/functions/restart_game/). The bug is already noted as existing by the review mandate; this finding records the COVERAGE GAP — a test asserting that a rematch room preserves `p.ai` on each bot seat would have caught this. Both other `players.map` call-sites on lines 40 and 184 of the same file also drop `ai`.
impact: After a rematch the new room's bot seats have no `ai` property. The referee's `authorizeAction` in validate.ts (line 207, 220) checks `players[actingSeatIndex].ai` to allow proxying; without the flag every human-proxy submission for a bot seat is rejected as 403 'Cannot act for another human player', soft-locking the rematch game.
recommendation: Add a Deno test file supabase/functions/restart_game/restart_game.test.ts with a pure unit test for the player-mapping logic: given an input roster containing a seat with ai:'medium', assert the output roster preserves ai:'medium'. Then fix the map to include `...(p.ai ? { ai: p.ai } : {})`.
acceptance_criteria: Test: build newPlayers from a roster containing a bot (ai:'medium'); assert newPlayers[i].ai === 'medium'
Test: build newPlayers from an all-human roster; assert no seat has an ai property
Test: the rematch wire response's players array is derived from newPlayers and carries ai (or not) correctly

title: finish_game sanitizeScoreboard has no test coverage
evidence: No test file exists anywhere under supabase/functions/finish_game/ (confirmed: only index.ts). The `sanitizeScoreboard` function contains 15 lines of non-trivial validation logic: tankId regex (/^p[1-9]\d*$/), seat-count bound check, coercion of numeric fields (roundWins/kills/totalDamage), playerName truncation at 40 chars, and the early-return null paths for malformed entries. The validate.test.ts and index.test.ts harnesses exist only for submit_action. `scoreboard.mjs` (read: checked whole file) tests the ENGINE-side kill/damage accumulation but asserts nothing about the Edge Function sanitizer.
impact: A regression in sanitizeScoreboard (e.g. an off-by-one on seat-count bounds, or a coercion path that accepts a negative roundWins) lands silently in production — the function returns { ok: true } regardless of whether sanitization ran (line 110-125). Because the function fails-open on bad scoreboards (best-effort), the match finishes but the match_scores table may store corrupted data or be skipped when it shouldn't be.
recommendation: Add supabase/functions/finish_game/finish_game.test.ts with pure unit tests exporting and importing sanitizeScoreboard: valid full board passes; board with p0/p99 tankId returns null; board with negative kills gets clamped to 0; playerName longer than 40 chars is truncated; rounds coercion (non-integer rounds → 1).
acceptance_criteria: Test: valid 2-seat scoreboard passes and returns clean entries
Test: tankId 'p0' returns null
Test: tankId index exceeding seatCount returns null
Test: negative kills/totalDamage are coerced to 0
Test: playerName > 40 chars is truncated to 40

title: authorizeAction has no test for out-of-rotation fire from an eliminated seat
evidence: Read validate.test.ts in full. The 10 authorizeAction tests cover: active human fires own shot (pass); next_round passes; wrong actingId (403); proxy-for-human blocked; proxy-for-bot pass; round-over buy cases (4 variants). NOT covered: a 3+ player game where the referee's stored `active_player_index` points to a seat index that the engine has since advanced past (i.e., the client reported a nextActiveIndex for an eliminated seat but the referee accepted it). The pure authorizeAction function does no liveness check — it only checks `players[activeIndex].id === actingId` — so if `activeIndex` in the DB is stale (e.g. due to a missed cursor update), the gate silently accepts a fire from the wrong player. The turn_rotation.mjs harness (read in full) proves the ENGINE skips dead seats; it does NOT test the referee's gating behavior when the cursor is at an eliminated index.
impact: In a 3-4P game, if `active_player_index` on the rooms row ever points to an eliminated seat (e.g. due to a race between the client-reported nextActiveIndex and a stale DB value), the gate's activePlayer is the dead seat and any submitter whose id happens to match that seat's id could fire out of turn. More likely the gate blocks everyone and soft-locks the game.
recommendation: Add authorizeAction tests with a 3-player roster where activeIndex points to a slot whose id does not match any alive player, and assert the gate rejects or passes correctly per regime. Also add a test where the reporter submits as actingId matching players[activeIndex] but activeIndex is at a dead seat (ghost seat scenario).
acceptance_criteria: Test: 3-player game, activeIndex=1 (eliminated, id 'uid-b'), submitter='uid-b' fires → 403 Not your turn (nobody should be able to act for a dead seat in normal flow)
Test: 4-player game, activeIndex=2 (dead), submitter='uid-c' fires → behavior is asserted unambiguously

title: 6 of 10 mutating edge functions have zero test coverage
evidence: Confirmed by filesystem glob: supabase/functions/ contains test files only for submit_action (index.test.ts, validate.test.ts), create_room (validate.test.ts), _shared (mod.test.ts), and list_rooms (list_rooms.test.ts). The following functions have NO .test.ts file: join_room, leave_room, ready_up, heartbeat, update_player, restart_game, finish_game. Each of these functions contains non-trivial validation and mutation logic. Specifically: join_room has lazy-GC reap logic + dead-room deletion; ready_up contains the all-ready-start transition; leave_room has the last-player-delete branch; restart_game has the atomic claim/race logic.
impact: Any regression in validation (e.g. join_room allowing a name longer than 20 chars, leave_room not deleting when empty) has no automated safety net. The ready_up all-ready transition and the restart_game race/claim pattern are particularly high-risk as they contain branchy state-machine logic.
recommendation: Extract pure validator/logic functions from these handlers (mirroring the validate.ts pattern in submit_action) and write hermetic Deno unit tests. Highest priority: ready_up (start condition), restart_game (claim race logic, ai-flag preservation), join_room (reap+capacity+conflict checks).
acceptance_criteria: ready_up: test allReady=true + enoughPlayers=true => started:true in response
ready_up: test single player ready => started:false
join_room: test capacity-full returns 409
join_room: test reap() of stale players before capacity check
restart_game: test ai-flag preserved on newPlayers

title: bump_rate_limit window rollover / concurrent increment has no unit test
evidence: Read mod.test.ts in full. Tests for rate limiting cover: checkRateLimit (under/at/over limit), clientIp (header parsing), rateWindow (same-60s-window and next-window), rateLimitFor (known bucket + default). NOT covered: (1) the window-rollover scenario — a bucket that had count=60 in window W is allowed (not denied) in window W+1 because it starts at count=1; (2) the concurrent-increment semantic — the SQL uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET count = rate_limits.count + 1, which is correct for serialization, but there is no test that the app-side checkRateLimit receives the post-increment count (not pre-). The enforceRateLimit function in mod.ts is async and calls the live DB — it has no injectable seam, so concurrent increment is only testable via integration test. The stale-window cleanup (DELETE WHERE window_start < p_window - 1) on line 52 of the SQL has no coverage.
impact: If the window-rollover logic is broken in the DB function (e.g. upsert key changed), a user who hit the limit in the previous window would be blocked forever. The stale-window cleanup bug would cause unbounded table growth. Neither would be caught before production.
recommendation: Add mod.test.ts cases: rateWindow rolls over correctly for edge ms values (59999ms vs 60000ms in the same epoch-minute block); checkRateLimit(1, 10) and checkRateLimit(limit, limit) and checkRateLimit(limit+1, limit) boundary is clean. Also add an integration test stub (even if skipped in CI) that calls bump_rate_limit twice in the same window and asserts the second count is 2.
acceptance_criteria: Test: rateWindow at the exact 60s boundary returns a different bucket than 1ms before
Test: checkRateLimit at exactly the limit is ALLOWED (inclusive boundary, read: this already exists — verify the rollover scenario is also asserted)
Test or documented manual check: bump_rate_limit(bucket, W) twice returns 1 then 2

title: nextCursor has no test for the eliminated-seat-reporting scenario (client reports a dead seat index)
evidence: Read mod.test.ts in full. The nextCursor tests cover 9 cases: valid reported honored; reported===activeIndex not round-over → modulo; round-over reported===activeIndex honored; out-of-bounds (>= playersLength); negative; null; turn increments; currentTurn=0; 2-player wrap; round-over + OOB. NOT covered: the scenario where a malicious or buggy client reports an index that corresponds to an eliminated seat — one that is in-bounds (0 <= reported < playersLength) and different from activeIndex, so it passes the `reportedValid` check, but the seat at that index is dead. nextCursor has no knowledge of alive/dead state (by design — it is a pure cursor function), but there is no test documenting/asserting this behavior and its consequence: a dead seat becomes the next active_player_index, causing the next turn's authorizeAction gate to expect that dead seat to fire.
impact: If a client reports a dead seat as the next index (malicious or buggy), the referee's cursor advances to the dead seat. The next call to authorizeAction compares players[activeIndex].id === actingId where activeIndex now points to an eliminated player — the dead player's id can never submit, soft-locking the game. The modulo fallback would safely advance past it, but the referee trusts the reported index whenever it is in-bounds.
recommendation: Add a test that documents the 'dead seat trust' behavior: nextCursor({activeIndex:0, playersLength:3, reported:1, isRoundOver:false, currentTurn:0}) returns index:1 (reported honored, no liveness check). This is expected behavior for a pure function, but pinning it documents the contract and alerts reviewers if the function ever adds liveness awareness.
acceptance_criteria: Test: reported in-bounds dead seat is honored (documents the pure-cursor contract)
Documentation or comment in mod.ts that nextCursor does not check liveness and callers must validate the reported index against alive seats if needed
